quiz_questions.question_text: In sports analytics, a player's batting average is an example of a:

options.text: Standard deviation
Measure of spread
Z-score
Proportion (hits divided by at-bats)

quiz_questions.explanation: Batting average = hits / at-bats, which is a proportion or rate, not a measure of central tendency like the arithmetic mean.

quiz_questions.question_text: Clinical trials use a control group to:

options.text: Make the study longer
Eliminate all variables
Increase the sample size artificially
Provide a baseline for comparison to determine whether a treatment has an effect

quiz_questions.explanation: A control group receives no treatment (or a placebo), providing a baseline against which the treatment group's outcomes are compared.

quiz_questions.question_text: The placebo effect in medical studies refers to:

options.text: The side effects of a drug that patients experience during the course of treatment
A medication that always works regardless of the patient's condition or expectations
Improvement in patients who receive an inactive treatment because they believe they are being treated
A statistical calculation error that arises from using the wrong formula in analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: The placebo effect occurs when patients experience real improvements simply because they believe they are receiving treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: In sports, the 'Moneyball' approach is known for using:

options.text: Intuition and tradition to evaluate players
Only home run statistics
Random player selection
Statistical analysis and data-driven decision making to build competitive teams

quiz_questions.explanation: The Moneyball approach, popularized by the Oakland Athletics, used statistics (sabermetrics) to find undervalued players and compete against wealthier teams.

quiz_questions.question_text: A double-blind clinical trial means:

options.text: The study uses two different drugs
Two trials are run simultaneously
Patients are tested twice
Neither the patients nor the researchers know who receives the treatment vs. placebo

quiz_questions.explanation: In a double-blind study, both patients and researchers are unaware of group assignments, minimizing bias in treatment and assessment.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital reports that patients with a certain surgery have a 95% survival rate. To interpret this properly, you should ask:

options.text: Over what time period, for what patient population, and how 'survival' is defined
The hospital's location, since geography has no effect on how outcomes are measured
Nothing; 95% is always good and requires no additional context to interpret properly
Only the surgeon's name, since individual reputation is the only relevant factor

quiz_questions.explanation: A 95% one-year survival rate is very different from a 95% five-year rate. Patient selection criteria and outcome definitions are critical context.

quiz_questions.question_text: In basketball, a player's 'Player Efficiency Rating' (PER) combines multiple stats. This is an example of:

options.text: A z-score that tells how many standard deviations a value falls from the overall mean
A single raw statistic that has not been combined with or adjusted by any other metric
A simple average of all the observed values in the dataset without any weighting applied
A composite index that aggregates multiple performance metrics into one number

quiz_questions.explanation: PER is a composite statistic that weights multiple performance categories (points, rebounds, assists, etc.) to summarize overall player effectiveness.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical study with a sample size of 15 finds a new drug reduces headaches by 40%. This result should be viewed with caution because:

options.text: Only percentages above 50% matter
The small sample size means the result has a wide margin of error and may not replicate
40% is too large to be real
Headache data cannot be collected

quiz_questions.explanation: With only 15 participants, the estimate is highly variable. A larger study is needed to confirm or refute the finding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soccer team has 5 players with salaries: $1M, $1.5M, $2M, $3M, $20M. Which statistic best represents the 'typical' player salary?

options.text: Mode, because there is no repeated value
Median ($2M), because it is not distorted by the $20M outlier
Range ($19M), because it shows the spread
Mean ($5.5M), because it uses all data

quiz_questions.explanation: The $20M star inflates the mean to $5.5M, which exceeds 4 of 5 salaries. The median ($2M) better represents the typical player.

quiz_questions.question_text: In epidemiology, 'incidence rate' measures:

options.text: The standard deviation of hospital stays
The average cost of treatment
The total number of people in a population
The number of new cases of a disease per unit of population per unit of time

quiz_questions.explanation: Incidence rate = new cases / (population at risk × time period). It measures how quickly disease is spreading.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'sabermetrics'?

options.text: The empirical analysis of baseball statistics to evaluate player performance and make strategic decisions
A method for calculating body mass index by dividing weight by the square of height
A type of sword-fighting measurement used in competitive fencing to track blade actions
A medical imaging technique that uses statistical models to visualize internal organs

quiz_questions.explanation: Sabermetrics (SABR = Society for American Baseball Research) applies statistical analysis to baseball data, pioneered by Bill James and popularized by Moneyball.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'randomized controlled trial' (RCT)?

options.text: A trial where the judge randomly selects jurors
An experiment where participants are randomly assigned to treatment or control groups to minimize bias
A survey with random questions
An observational study with no intervention

quiz_questions.explanation: RCTs are the gold standard in medical research. Random assignment ensures treatment groups are comparable, allowing causal conclusions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'on-base percentage' (OBP) measure in baseball?

options.text: The number of home runs per game, which measures a batter's power-hitting frequency
The proportion of plate appearances in which a batter reaches base safely (via hit, walk, or hit-by-pitch)
The fielding error rate, which tracks how often a defensive player misplays the ball
The batting average only, calculated by dividing total hits by total at-bat opportunities

quiz_questions.explanation: OBP = (Hits + Walks + HBP) / (At-bats + Walks + HBP + Sacrifice flies). It is more comprehensive than batting average.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'survival rate' mean in medicine?

options.text: The cost of surgery, including all medical expenses related to the procedure performed
The percentage of patients who are alive after a specified period following diagnosis or treatment
The number of patients admitted to a hospital during a specified calendar time period
The length of a hospital stay, measured in days from admission through final discharge

quiz_questions.explanation: Survival rate is typically expressed as a percentage surviving after a defined period (e.g., 5-year survival rate for cancer patients).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'regression to the mean' in sports context?

options.text: The team's win percentage always approaching 50%
A player's performance always getting worse over time
The tendency for extreme performances to be followed by more typical ones closer to the player's long-term average
A statistical formula for predicting home runs

quiz_questions.explanation: Regression to the mean explains why a player who has an exceptional season often performs closer to their career average the following year.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'relative risk' mean in medical studies?

options.text: The ratio of the probability of an event in the treatment group to its probability in the control group
The total number of side effects reported during the clinical trial observation period
The cost of medical treatment, including drugs, procedures, and all associated expenses
A patient's personal risk tolerance, which reflects their willingness to accept side effects

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative risk compares the incidence of an outcome between two groups. RR = 2.0 means the treatment group is twice as likely to experience the outcome.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'Wins Above Replacement' (WAR) in sports analytics?

options.text: A player's total number of wins accumulated over the course of an entire career or season
The number of wars a country has fought, which is a historical measure unrelated to sports
The difference between wins and losses, also known as a team's overall margin of victory
A composite statistic estimating how many more wins a player contributes compared to a replacement-level (average) player

quiz_questions.explanation: WAR estimates a player's total contribution by measuring how many additional wins they provide over what a freely available replacement player would contribute.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'number needed to treat' (NNT) mean?

options.text: The number of patients who must receive a treatment for one additional patient to benefit compared to the control group
The total number of patients in a hospital at any given point during the study period
The number of doses required to complete the full prescribed course of treatment
The number of doctors per patient, which reflects the staffing ratio at a medical facility

quiz_questions.explanation: NNT = 1 / (absolute risk reduction). A lower NNT indicates a more effective treatment. NNT = 10 means treating 10 patients benefits 1 additional patient.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'load management' in sports, from a statistical perspective?

options.text: Loading equipment onto trucks for transportation to the stadium before game day events
Using data on player fatigue, injury risk, and performance metrics to strategically rest players for optimal long-term performance
Reducing the weight of player uniforms to improve comfort and range of motion on field
Managing the team's budget and financial resources to stay within the salary cap limits

quiz_questions.explanation: Load management uses statistical models of injury risk, cumulative workload, and performance trends to decide when to rest players.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'absolute risk reduction' (ARR) mean?

options.text: The relative risk divided by the sample size
The standard deviation of treatment outcomes
The total number of adverse events
The difference in event rates between the control group and the treatment group

quiz_questions.explanation: ARR = control event rate − treatment event rate. If the control has 20% events and treatment has 15%, ARR = 5 percentage points.

quiz_questions.question_text: A baseball player hit .350 in April (50 at-bats) but only .260 for the full season (500 at-bats). A fan argues April showed his 'true talent.' Why is the full-season average more reliable?

options.text: Larger sample sizes produce more reliable estimates; the hot April likely reflected random variation (regression to the mean)
April data should be excluded because it's spring training
Both averages are equally reliable
Full-season averages are always lower

quiz_questions.explanation: With only 50 at-bats, random variation can produce extreme batting averages. Over 500 at-bats, the average converges closer to true ability (regression to the mean).

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company advertises that its new statin reduces heart attack risk by 50%. The actual data: 2 out of 100 patients on placebo had a heart attack vs. 1 out of 100 on the statin. Is '50% reduction' misleading?

options.text: No, 50% is accurate and impressive enough to communicate the benefit to patients clearly
The study is invalid because the sample is too large and may detect trivially small effects
No, relative risk always tells the full story and absolute risk adds no useful information
Yes, the relative risk reduction is 50%, but the absolute risk reduction is only 1 percentage point (from 2% to 1%), which is less dramatic

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative risk reduction (50%) sounds dramatic, but the absolute risk reduction (2%→1% = 1 percentage point) shows the actual benefit is modest. NNT = 100.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sports team's analytics department tracks a midfielder's running distance, sprint count, pass accuracy, and tackles per game. Over 10 games, pass accuracy drops from 88% to 79% while running distance stays constant. What might the data suggest?

options.text: The player is performing equally well since distance is unchanged
The player may be fatigued or injured: maintaining distance but losing precision suggests declining effectiveness that a single stat would miss
The analytics are wrong because running distance didn't change
Pass accuracy is irrelevant for midfielders

quiz_questions.explanation: Multiple metrics tell a richer story. A decline in precision alongside stable endurance metrics may indicate fatigue, minor injury, or tactical problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: A vaccine trial reports: Treatment group (10,000 people): 5 got the disease. Placebo group (10,000 people): 100 got the disease. What is the vaccine's efficacy?

A basketball analyst compares two players. Player A: 25 points per game, 3 turnovers per game. Player B: 18 points per game, 1 turnover per game. The analyst says Player A is clearly better. What statistical context is being overlooked?

options.text: Player A plays more minutes so the comparison is always valid and no adjustment is needed
Efficiency: Player B may have a higher points-per-possession ratio or fewer turnovers per minute, meaning Player B uses possessions more effectively
Nothing; more points always means better performance regardless of efficiency or context
Only rebounds matter when evaluating overall basketball player effectiveness and value

quiz_questions.explanation: Raw totals don't account for usage, minutes played, or efficiency. Rate statistics and composite metrics provide better context for player comparison.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital implements a new hand-washing protocol. Before the protocol, the infection rate was 8 per 1,000 patients. After, it dropped to 3 per 1,000. The NNT (number needed to treat) for the hand-washing protocol is:
quiz_questions.explanation: ARR = 8/1000 − 3/1000 = 5/1000 = 0.005. NNT = 1/0.005 = 200. For every 200 patients exposed to the protocol, one infection is prevented.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marathon runner's finishing times over 10 races are: 3:10, 3:08, 3:15, 3:12, 3:11, 3:09, 3:14, 3:10, 3:13, 3:11 (hours:minutes). The coach says the runner is 'very consistent.' Which statistic best supports this claim?

options.text: The mean time of about 3:11, which simply reports the average without any variability context
The fastest time of 3:08, which is a single extreme value and does not represent consistency
The small standard deviation (about 2 minutes) and low CV relative to the mean
The range of 7 minutes, which only captures the gap between the fastest and slowest races

quiz_questions.explanation: Consistency is measured by low variability. A standard deviation of only ~2 minutes across 10 races demonstrates high consistency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A clinical study reports that a new diabetes medication reduces HbA1c by 0.3% compared to placebo (p < 0.001). Doctors consider a 0.5% reduction clinically meaningful. What is the correct interpretation?

options.text: Statistical significance always implies clinical significance so both conditions are always met
The drug should be approved immediately since p < 0.001 proves it is effective for patients
The drug is highly effective because p < 0.001 is an extremely strong level of significance
The result is statistically significant but NOT clinically significant, as the 0.3% reduction falls below the 0.5% threshold doctors consider meaningful

quiz_questions.explanation: A very low p-value means the effect is unlikely due to chance, but the effect size (0.3%) is too small to be clinically useful. Context matters.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sports journalist claims a quarterback had a 'career year' based solely on total passing yards. However, the QB also had the most interceptions in the league. What principle of data interpretation does this illustrate?

options.text: Total yards is always the most important stat and overrides all other performance measures
One statistic tells the complete story and multiple metrics are unnecessary for evaluation
Evaluating performance requires multiple metrics; cherry-picking one favorable stat while ignoring negatives creates a misleading narrative
Interceptions don't matter in football because they are not part of overall quarterback ratings

quiz_questions.explanation: Comprehensive evaluation requires looking at multiple dimensions. High yards with high turnovers suggests volume without efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: A health study compares mortality rates in two groups: exercisers (n=5,000) and non-exercisers (n=5,000). Exercisers had a 2% mortality rate vs. 5% for non-exercisers. Before concluding that exercise prevents death, what must be considered?

options.text: The sample size is too large, which means the study detected only trivially small effects
This is observational, so confounders (age, baseline health, socioeconomic status, diet) may explain the difference; exercisers may be healthier to begin with
Nothing; the data prove exercise prevents death and no further interpretation is required
Only a p-value is needed to make a causal claim about the benefits of regular exercise

quiz_questions.explanation: Observational studies cannot establish causation. People who exercise regularly may also eat better, be younger, or have better access to healthcare—all confounding variables.
